title: Central concerns in social stratification research: comments on Goldthorpe
abstract: John H. Goldthorpe's work on social stratification has been central to British sociology for at least forty years and it has had a huge impact internationally. This international impact has been most notable within Europe, where Goldthorpe has led and participated in a number of collaborative projects and investigations. His work constitutes one of the leading paradigms in social stratification research, its principal rivals being the Marxist framework of Erik Olin Wright (see, for example Wright 1985; Wright et al. 1989), and the more cultural framework inspired by Pierre Bourdieu (Bourdieu and Passeron 1970; Bourdieu 1979). The impact of his work has been felt beyond the area of stratification studies, and his arguments have often engendered controversy. His views on the relations between gender and class, for example, have been the targets of attack by feminist critics (see Stanworth 1984 commenting on Goldthorpe 1983; and see Goldthorpe 1984). Goldthorpe's approach is broadly Weberian, although not uncritically so. His general framework was developed in collaboration with David Lockwood, whose own concern for structural and systemic issues tempered Goldthorpe's strong methodological individualism – an individualism that led him to emphasize strongly the relevance of rational action theory for sociological explanation (Goldthorpe 1998). The outlines of the theoretical framework were developed in papers of the early 1960s (Goldthorpe and Lockwood 1963; Goldthorpe 1964), and culminated in research reported for the project on the ‘Affluent Worker’ (Goldthorpe et al. 1968a; Goldthorpe et al. 1968b; Goldthorpe et al. 1969). The theoretical framework centred on a distinction between class situation and status situation as the two key dimensions of social stratification. For Goldthorpe, class and status divisions comprise the causal determinants of life chances, and social strata or social classes are seen as demographically formed groupings of individuals with similar causal determinants operating to shape their life Chances. Goldthorpe's work proposes that social stratification can, therefore, be mapped as a hierarchical structure of social classes, which in turn can be analysed in terms of the structural determinants that underpin them. Since the 1970s, Goldthorpe's work has involved a more direct investigation of occupational mobility, seen as the principal aspect in social mobility.1 Goldthorpe asserts that patterns of social mobility, traced by individuals as they move from one class situation to another, constitute a key element in the demographic formation of social classes. In what came to be known as the Oxford Mobility Study, Goldthorpe and his colleagues devised a scheme of occupational gradings that could be used as measures of class structure (Goldthorpe and Hope 1974; and see Hope 1972; Ridge 1974), and they employed increasingly complex and powerful statistical methods to map occupational distributions and to measure the movement from one occupational grouping to another (Goldthorpe 1987; and see Halsey et al. 1980). This work has subsequently been extended into comparative investigations that have sought to generalize the occupational schema in ways that would make it more useful internationally, and to revise and update the schema to reflect Changes in occupational structure (Erikson and Goldthorpe 1992).2 Goldthorpe's efforts have been central to the construction of a new scheme of classification designed to replace the Registrar General's scale for official investigations using census and registration data (Rose and O'Reilly 1997). This scheme of classification was, paradoxically, developed at a time when governments were turning their backs on measures of social class. The Goldthorpe paper featured here in this special anniversary issue, ‘Class Analysis and the Reorientation of Class Theory’ (2010 [1996]), was first presented at a conference at Essex University to mark the retirement of David Lockwood, and it was originally published in a 1996 special issue of the British Journal of Sociology in honour of Lockwood's work. The article is notable because it dates from what was, perhaps, the most productive and innovative phase of Goldthorpe's work, and it represents an important attempt to use rational action theory to explore educational decision-making and attainment and their implications for class reproduction. The paper takes a voluntaristic ‘action frame of reference’, as set out by Parsons (1937; and see Rex 1961: ch. 2), but limits this to a consideration of rational or strategic action. Eschewing the extremes of some forms of rational choice theory, Goldthorpe seeks to outline a model of the rational, calculating actor whose commitment to social norms (including those rooted in status differences) frequently limits the extent to which they pursue this rationality. Goldthorpe specifically argues that actors adopt means that are appropriate to their goals and act within the constraints – the opportunities and limits – that are determined by the resources inherent in their class situations. Class differences in educational attainment persist across the generations, Goldthorpe argues, because parents in disadvantaged class situations make rational decisions that lead their children to leave the educational system earlier than those from more advantaged class situations. They calculate that their life chances will not be significantly enhanced by continued participation in the educational system. Goldthorpe rejects, therefore, the arguments of those such as Paul Willis (1977) who see class cultures as the determinants of class reproduction, although Fiona Devine (2004) has drawn on the work of Bourdieu to show that these influences cannot be disregarded quite so easily. Indeed, Goldthorpe's recent work has also taken a cultural turn, taking up the importance of culture alongside the material facts of class situation. This has been pursued through a move from class analysis to the investigation of status. Working with Tak Wing Chan, Goldthorpe has more recently argued for a recognition of the importance of Weber's distinction between class and status, which had been central to his own work of the 1960s (Chan and Goldthorpe 2007). Goldthorpe now uses the idea of status and the status order to explore issues of cultural consumption. Strangely, Goldthorpe had, at the Lockwood retirement conference at Essex, criticized those who constantly went back to Weber and his conceptual distinctions, yet his recent work not only draws such distinctions but also argues that they have been ignored or underemphasized by other sociologists.3 Goldthorpe's paradigm for social stratification research – exemplified in the paper featured in this special issue – has a continuing relevance for work in the field. It does not, however, provide the whole picture. Complementary themes have been raised in the numerous debates with his critics, and by Goldthorpe's own political rapprochement with cultural theories of status and normative commitment, that emphasize the analytical power to be gained by combining Goldthorpe's approach with aspects of the work of Bourdieu. Such a rapprochement is manifest, in even more visible form, by the work of Mike Savage. Savage has moved from an early advocacy of Goldthorpe and rejection of cultural explanation (Savage, Watt and Arber 1990; see also Savage 1987), through an attempt to combine this with elements from the work of Wright and Bourdieu (Savage et al. 1992), to more recent investigations into the significance of cultural differences (Savage 2000). The emerging framework of social stratification analysis is far from being a neat theoretical synthesis, and many differences of emphasis and substance remain (see the essays in Devine et al. 2004; and the more general accounts in Wright 2005). There are, however, signs of genuine intellectual engagement and advance, and Goldthorpe's work will continue to be central to this.
